title: Underfitting vs. Overfitting: The Two Failure Modes
concept: Underfitting is a model too simple to catch the real pattern; overfitting is a model so complex it memorizes noise instead. Good models sit in the goldilocks zone between the two.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does it mean when a model overfits?

options.text: It memorizes quirks of the training data instead of learning the general pattern
It refuses to make any predictions at all
It trains too quickly and finishes early

exercises.explain: Right — you've met overfitting already. Today: it turns out there's an opposite failure too.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model can also fail by being too simple, not just by being too complex.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! That failure mode has a name too: underfitting. Let's meet it.

cards.title: Goldilocks and the model
cards.body: One porridge is too plain and bland — it misses the point entirely. Another is stuffed with so many random ingredients it tastes like nothing in particular. The one Goldilocks actually wants sits right in between: just enough flavor, nothing extra. Models have the same three zones.

cards.title: What is underfitting?
cards.body: Underfitting is when a model is too simple to capture the real pattern in the data. It performs poorly on training data and on new data, because it never learned enough in the first place.

cards.title: Three students, three outcomes
cards.body: Student A barely skims the chapter titles and misses the whole subject — that's underfitting. Student B memorizes every word of the textbook without understanding it — that's overfitting. Student C actually understands the concepts well enough to handle new questions — that's the goldilocks zone: a good fit.

cards.title: Pricing houses, two ways
cards.body: An underfit house-price model just guesses the same average price for every house, ignoring size or location entirely — wrong almost everywhere. An overfit model memorizes the exact price of every house it saw, down to quirky one-off deals — perfect on those houses, wrong on any new one.

cards.title: More complexity isn't automatically better
cards.body: It's tempting to think a more complicated model always wins. But past a certain point, extra complexity doesn't add understanding — it just starts memorizing noise, sliding straight from a good fit into overfitting.

cards.title: Two failure modes, one spectrum
cards.body: Both ends of the spectrum fail — for opposite reasons:

left.text:
Too simple
Misses the pattern
Bad on training AND new data

right.text:
Too complex
Memorizes noise
Great on training, bad on new data

exercises.prompt: A model scores poorly on BOTH its training data and new test data. What does this suggest?

options.text: The model is likely underfitting — too simple to capture the pattern at all
The model is overfitting badly
The model has achieved a perfect goldilocks fit
The training data must be corrupted

exercises.explain: Exactly — poor scores on both sets (not just a big gap between them) is the signature of underfitting.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Underfitting and overfitting are actually the same problem with different names.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! They're opposite failures — one from too little learning (underfitting), one from too much memorizing (overfitting).

exercises.sentence: A model that is too ___ to capture the real pattern is underfitting.
exercises.explain: Underfitting comes from a model that's too simple to learn the pattern in the first place.

exercises.prompt: Which of these describes an underfit model?

options.text: A model that predicts the same flat price for every house, regardless of size, location, or condition
A model that nails every training house's price perfectly but is way off on any new house
A model that performs consistently well on both training and new houses

exercises.explain: Right! Ignoring the real differences between houses and guessing one flat answer is a classic underfitting symptom.

exercises.prompt: Where does the best-performing model usually sit, in terms of complexity?

options.text: In the goldilocks zone — complex enough to learn the real pattern, not so complex it memorizes noise
At maximum possible complexity, always
At minimum possible complexity, always
Complexity has no effect on performance

exercises.explain: Exactly — too simple underfits, too complex overfits, and the sweet spot is in between.

exercises.prompt: Model X scores 55% on training data and 53% on test data. Model Y scores 99% on training data and 58% on test data. How should you describe each?

options.text: Model X is underfitting (low everywhere); Model Y is overfitting (huge gap between training and test)
Both models are overfitting equally
Model X is overfitting; Model Y is underfitting
Both models have achieved a perfect goldilocks fit

exercises.explain: Exactly! Low-everywhere scores point to underfitting; a huge train-vs-test gap points to overfitting.

exercises.prompt: A team's plant-disease model underfits, so they make it dramatically more complex to fix it. Now training accuracy is 100%, but test accuracy dropped from 60% to 45%. What happened?

options.text: They overcorrected — the model swung past the goldilocks zone straight into overfitting
The model is now perfectly fixed, since training accuracy is 100%
The model is still underfitting
Test accuracy dropping is unrelated to the complexity change

exercises.explain: Right! Adding complexity to fix underfitting can easily overshoot into overfitting — that's why the goldilocks zone matters so much.
